name: Raudondvaris Manor
region: Kaunas District, Nevėžis River, ~9km northwest of Kaunas
address: Raudondvario g. 78, Raudondvaris, Kaunas District, Lithuania
architectural_style: Gothic Revival red-brick manor over a Teutonic castle core — the site's construction history runs from a pagan hillfort (first recorded 1392) to the Teutonic Order's Königsburg (a garrisoned castle of 80 knights and 400 soldiers), through a Renaissance manor built for Queen Barbara Radziwiłł in 1549, to the Gothic Revival brick mansion built 1832–1855 by Count Emmanuel Benedykt Tyszkiewicz; a round tower on the southern side is believed to be a physical survival from the Teutonic-period construction, retained through all subsequent rebuilds; the name 'Raudondvaris' (Red Manor) derives from the red brick that defines the building at every phase of its history; the Gothic Revival remodel produced steeply pitched roofs, pointed windows, and battlemented parapets typical of the 19th-century romantic medievalism that characterized aristocratic building across Central and Eastern Europe

opening_hours.seasonal_note: Raudondvaris Manor is primarily open in the main tourist season (May–September). Opening hours and admission vary by season and event programming; confirm current access via the Kaunas District tourism portal before visiting. The GYG Nemunas River Castles tour from Vilnius (t1402021, from $227, 10 hours, max 7 guests) includes a dedicated guided stop at the manor — book via GYG for a guaranteed access and guide.

visit_duration: 45 minutes–1 hour (manor and grounds); 10 hours (GYG full Nemunas River Castles circuit from Vilnius)
best_season: May to September — the manor's exterior and grounds are best appreciated in clear weather; the surrounding Nevėžis River valley is an attractive landscape context for the visit
accessibility: The manor grounds are broadly accessible. Interior access depends on current programming. Confirm specific accessibility with the site.
tagline: From Teutonic knights' garrison to Queen Barbara Radziwiłł's manor — a property that passed through the hands of the Grand Duchy's most powerful queen before an aristocratic family filled it with old masters
highlights: Queen Barbara Radziwiłł's manor (1549) — Sigismund II Augustus, King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania, gave the property to his wife Barbara Radziwiłł in 1549; she had the red-brick manor built that gave the surrounding village its name (Raudondvaris = Red Manor); Barbara Radziwiłł was the most powerful woman in the Grand Duchy, whose marriage to Sigismund caused a constitutional crisis and whose death in 1551 inspired enduring Polish-Lithuanian cultural memory

The Teutonic Order's Königsburg — before becoming a royal manor, the site was a Teutonic Order castle built after 1392, garrisoned with 80 knights and 400 soldiers; the name Königsburg (King's Castle in German) reflects its status as one of the Order's primary installations in the contested border territories between Prussia and Lithuania

The Tyszkiewicz family's old-master art collection — Count Emmanuel Benedykt Tyszkiewicz acquired the manor in 1819 and rebuilt it as a Gothic Revival residence (1832–1855); the Tyszkiewicz family used the manor to house an art collection that reportedly included works attributed to Leonardo da Vinci, Rubens, Caravaggio, and Jan Matejko — an extraordinary concentration of old-master painting in a provincial Lithuanian manor that was dispersed during the upheavals of the 20th century

The surviving Teutonic tower — a round tower on the southern side of the complex is believed to predate the 16th-century manor and to be a physical survival from the Teutonic-period Königsburg, retained through the Renaissance and Gothic Revival rebuilds; if confirmed, it is the oldest visible architectural element on the site

GYG Kaunas & Nemunas River Castles day tour from Vilnius (t1402021, from $227, max 7 guests, 10 hours) — includes a dedicated guided stop at Raudondvaris Manor alongside Kaunas Castle, Raudonė Castle, and Panemunė Castle
description:
The name Raudondvaris means Red Manor in Lithuanian — a name that describes the building at every period of its history, since red brick has been the primary construction material at this site from the Teutonic Order's 14th-century fortification through the 16th-century royal manor and the 19th-century aristocratic rebuild. Raudondvaris is a place where the layers of Lithuanian history — medieval, royal, aristocratic, and Soviet — have accumulated in a single property on the banks of the Nevėžis River, 9 kilometres northwest of Kaunas.

The site's documented history begins in 1392, when the surrounding region of Samogitia was ceded to the Teutonic Order as part of the Ostrów Agreement — a treaty in which the Grand Duke of Lithuania temporarily surrendered the western Lithuanian territories to the Order in exchange for support against a rival claimant to the Lithuanian throne. The Teutonic Order, finding itself in possession of a strategically located hilltop above the Nevėžis River, built a castle called Königsburg (King's Castle in German): a garrisoned installation with 80 knights and 400 soldiers, equipped to serve as both a military position and an administrative center for the Order's new territory.

The Teutonic Order's control of Samogitia lasted until 1411 — the aftermath of the Battle of Grunwald, when the Order's decisive defeat forced the return of the territory to Lithuania under the terms of the Peace of Thorn. The Königsburg became Lithuanian property, and the site gradually transitioned from a military installation to a royal manor. Over the following century, ownership moved through the Kings of Poland and Grand Dukes of Lithuania, making Raudondvaris part of the royal domain rather than a private noble possession.

The most historically resonant episode in the manor's royal period came in 1549, when Sigismund II Augustus, King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania, gave the property to his wife Barbara Radziwiłł. The gift had a specific meaning: Barbara Radziwiłł was already one of the most powerful women in the Grand Duchy before her marriage to Sigismund, and the marriage itself had generated a constitutional crisis — the Polish-Lithuanian nobility had objected strenuously to a king marrying a Lithuanian noble rather than a foreign princess, and the Sejm (parliament) had attempted to block the marriage for years before Sigismund prevailed. Barbara's ownership of Raudondvaris was brief: she died in 1551, two years after receiving it, at the age of approximately 30. Her death inspired a long tradition of Polish-Lithuanian cultural commemoration, including plays, novels, and operas about the beautiful queen whose marriage to the king the nobility resented and whose early death inspired grief that lasted for decades.

It was Barbara who had the red-brick manor built that gave the surrounding village its name — the manor that replaced whatever remained of the Teutonic-era Königsburg and established the residential character of the site as distinct from its military origins. After Barbara's death, the property returned to royal hands and continued to circulate through the Lithuanian-Polish noble establishment over the following centuries.

The Tyszkiewicz family acquired the manor in 1819 and defined its modern appearance. Count Emmanuel Benedykt Tyszkiewicz commissioned the comprehensive rebuild of 1832–1855 that produced the Gothic Revival brick mansion currently standing — steeply pitched roofs, pointed window arches, battlemented parapets, and the romantic medievalism typical of 19th-century aristocratic building across Central and Eastern Europe. A round tower on the southern side of the complex is believed to be a physical survival from the Teutonic-period Königsburg, retained through the Renaissance and Gothic Revival rebuilds as the oldest visible element of the site's construction history.

The Tyszkiewicz family's most extraordinary contribution to Raudondvaris was cultural rather than architectural. Over the course of the 19th century, the family assembled an art collection at the manor that was reportedly one of the most significant in the Grand Duchy of Lithuania — including works attributed to Leonardo da Vinci, Rubens, Caravaggio, and the Polish historical painter Jan Matejko. The provenance and current whereabouts of most of these works are uncertain; the collection was dispersed during the upheavals of the First World War, the Russian occupation, and the Soviet period, and the manor itself was nationalized in 1915 when the Tyszkiewicz family fled to escape the advancing Russian front. The family had occupied the manor from 1819 to 1915 — 96 years of continuous aristocratic habitation and cultural accumulation that the 20th century undid in a matter of weeks.

Raudondvaris Manor is the second stop on the Nemunas River Castles circuit, a chain of four fortifications and manor houses in the Nemunas and Nevėžis river valleys that constitutes Lithuania's most concentrated medieval and early modern architectural sequence. [Kaunas Castle](/castles/lithuania/kaunas-castle), 9 kilometres downstream at the Nemunas-Neris confluence, is the circuit's starting point and the first brick castle of the Grand Duchy. [Raudonė Castle](/castles/lithuania/raudone-castle), further down the Nemunas, preserves a 34-metre cylindrical tower from its Renaissance phase. [Panemunė Castle](/castles/lithuania/panemune-castle), the westernmost of the four, was built from 1604 and confiscated by the Tsarist authorities after its owner led the failed November Uprising of 1830.

For Lithuania's other principal fortifications: [Trakai Island Castle](/castles/lithuania/trakai-island-castle) in the lake district 90 kilometres northeast is the Grand Duchy's most visually dramatic medieval castle; [Gediminas Tower](/castles/lithuania/gediminas-tower) in Vilnius is the upper castle of the Grand Duchy's capital.
history: 1392: Site first recorded — the region of Samogitia ceded to the Teutonic Order; the Order builds 'Königsburg' castle on the hilltop above the Nevėžis, garrisoned with 80 knights and 400 soldiers. 1411: Battle of Grunwald and Peace of Thorn return Samogitia to Lithuania; Königsburg becomes Lithuanian royal property. 15th–16th century: Property circulates through the Polish-Lithuanian royal domain. 1549: Sigismund II Augustus gives the manor to Queen Barbara Radziwiłł; she has the red-brick manor built that names the surrounding village 'Raudondvaris' (Red Manor). 1551: Barbara Radziwiłł dies; property returns to royal hands. 16th–19th century: Various owners; property remains part of the noble estate system. 1819: Tyszkiewicz family acquires the manor. 1831: Fire destroys remaining wooden structures. 1832–1855: Count Emmanuel Benedykt Tyszkiewicz rebuilds the manor in Gothic Revival style; notable art collection assembled. 1915: Tyszkiewicz family flees the advancing Russian front; manor nationalized. Soviet period: Manor used for various institutional purposes. Post-1990: Restored and opened for tourism in independent Lithuania.
how_to_visit:
**Direct visit:** Raudondvaris is 9km from central Kaunas by car — approximately 15 minutes; it can also be reached by local bus from Kaunas. Confirm current opening hours before visiting.

**GYG Nemunas River Castles day tour from Vilnius (~$227, t1402021, 10 hours, max 7 guests):** Includes a dedicated guided stop at Raudondvaris Manor alongside the other three Nemunas valley sites. Departs from Vilnius; lunch included.

**Combine with:** Kaunas Castle (9km east), Raudonė Castle (60km southwest), Panemunė Castle (80km west).

faqs.question: Who was Queen Barbara Radziwiłł and why does she matter to this manor?
faqs.answer: Barbara Radziwiłł (c.1520–1551) was a Lithuanian noblewoman who became the second wife of Sigismund II Augustus, King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania. Their marriage was deeply controversial: the Polish-Lithuanian nobility objected to a king marrying a Lithuanian noble rather than a foreign princess, and the Sejm attempted to block the marriage for years. Sigismund prevailed, and in 1549 he gave Raudondvaris to Barbara — it was at this point that the red-brick manor she commissioned named the surrounding village. Barbara died two years later in 1551, at approximately age 30, and her early death inspired a lasting cultural legacy in Polish and Lithuanian literature and opera. Her brief ownership of Raudondvaris connects this Nevėžis River manor to one of the most dramatic dynastic episodes in the Grand Duchy's history.

faqs.question: What happened to the Tyszkiewicz family's art collection?
faqs.answer: The Tyszkiewicz family's collection, reportedly including works attributed to Leonardo da Vinci, Rubens, Caravaggio, and Jan Matejko, was dispersed after the family fled in 1915 to escape the advancing Russian front during World War I. The manor was nationalized and the collection scattered through a combination of wartime displacement, Soviet-era redistribution, and the general destruction of the aristocratic estate system across the Baltic states. The current locations of most individual works are uncertain.

hero_image.alt: Raudondvaris Manor — the Gothic Revival red-brick manor house on the Nevėžis River, Kaunas District, Lithuania, rebuilt 1832–1855 by Count Tyszkiewicz on the site of the Teutonic Order's Königsburg and Queen Barbara Radziwiłł's Renaissance manor
